metadata.title: The Paper Trail in the Studio: How Music Gets Made

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The question of who gets paid when AI makes music is not rhetorical or a mere estimate. The move-fast phase ended because, at scale, the disruption is not marginal. Decades of research converged on converting audio into discrete tokens representing compressed sound chunks. Transformers predict the next token, so the architecture that predicts the next word predicts the next sound fragment.

artifactLines: The question of who gets paid when AI makes music is not rhetorical or a mere estimate.
The move-fast phase ended because, at scale, the disruption is not marginal.
Decades of research converged on converting audio into discrete tokens representing compressed sound chunks.
Transformers predict the next token, so the architecture that predicts the next word predicts the next sound fragment.

beats.narration_text: The move that stayed with me is converting audio into discrete tokens and letting a Transformer predict the next sound fragment, exactly like next-word prediction. I want to feel that equivalence concretely. Help me: (1) walk through how a few seconds of audio becomes a token sequence; (2) show why the same next-token objective transfers from sentences to song fragments; (3) reason through where this scales badly enough that 'move fast' had to end for the music industry.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: That is not a rhetorical question. That is not an estimate of disruption.

beats.narration_text: The “move fast” phase ended. At scale, this is not marginal.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The “move fast” phase ended. At scale, this is not marginal.

beats.narration_text: The answer, developed across decades of academic research, was to convert audio into discrete tokens—numbers that represent compressed audio chunks—and train Transformer models to predict which token comes next, given everything that came before. The same architecture that predicts the next word in a sentence predicts the next fragment of sound in a song.
